text: The threat actor ran 'tracert.exe 8.8.8.8' to test network reachability to the C2 server.
During C0017, APT41 used VMProtect to slow the reverse engineering of malicious binaries.
NETEAGLE can use HTTP to download resources that contain an IP address and port number pair to connect to for C2.
An attacker exploited email compromise to send fake payment instructions to the accounting department.
The threat actor performed network reconnaissance using nmap to discover subnets and routing information.
A persistence mechanism was established via 'reg add HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run /v UpdateService /t REG_SZ /d C:\malware.exe /f' to run malicious code at startup.
Empire contains modules for executing commands over SSH as well as in-memory VNC agent injection.
Grandoreiro has added BMP images to the resources section of its Portable Executable (PE) file increasing each binary to at least 300MB in size.
Naikon has used schtasks.exe for lateral movement in compromised networks.
After exfiltrating data, the attacker used 'net use D: /delete' to remove the mapped drive.
FruitFly malware was executed to secretly record images from the infected system's webcam.
The threat actor utilized Azure Serial Console to access a virtual machine's serial port for diagnostic and exploitation purposes.
Green Lambert can create a Launch Agent with the `RunAtLoad` key-value pair set to true, ensuring the `com.apple.GrowlHelper.plist` file runs every time a user logs in.
Sliver has the ability to manipulate user tokens on targeted Windows systems.
APT39 has used malware to drop encrypted CAB files.
Using net user, the malware established a secondary account named 'svc_maint' with administrative privileges.
Hannotog can stop Windows services.
More_eggs has used HTTP GET requests to check internet connectivity.
The malicious script set COR_PROFILER_PATH to point to a compromised DLL for execution within .NET runtime environments.
AADInternals can create new Azure AD users.
Malware persistence was achieved by writing a trojanized application to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run.
Malicious actors leveraged FTP protocol for C2 communications to avoid detection by traditional network monitoring.
The attacker modified HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Environment to load a malicious DLL into .NET processes.
FIN13 has used `nslookup` and `ipconfig` for network reconnaissance efforts. FIN13 has also utilized a compromised Symantec Altiris console and LanDesk account to retrieve network information.
StrelaStealer variants use COM objects to enumerate installed applications from the "AppsFolder" on victim machines.
The adversary established an IMAP connection to query and download email attachments from the victim's account.
The attacker modified Normal.dotm to execute malicious code whenever Word documents are opened.
The script queried the victim's careers page to identify technologies and roles used within the organization.
An adversary scanned Slack Workflow definitions to locate exposed AWS access keys.
C2 communications were hidden by dynamically changing URI paths between requests.
The malicious process ran 'reconnect_tool.exe' to restore communication with the command infrastructure.
The spyware employed HTTPS for C2 traffic to blend with legitimate web traffic.
The script contained 'echo "" > ~/.bash_history' to overwrite and erase all command history entries.

Latrodectus can determine if it is running in a virtualized environment by checking the OS version, checking the number of running processes, ensuring a 64-bit application is running on a 64-bit host, and checking if the host has a valid MAC address.
Malware injected a malicious.dll into the legitimate application to manipulate its data processing.
StrelaStealer has been delivered via JScript files in a ZIP archive.
TA2541 has hosted malicious files on various platforms including Google Drive, OneDrive, Discord, PasteText, ShareText, and GitHub.
The threat actor edited the authorized_keys file to include a backdoor SSH key for future access.
The threat actor ran pwdumpx.exe with administrative privileges to obtain local account NTLM hashes.
The threat actor configured HTRAN to relay encrypted C2 traffic through compromised intermediate systems.
The attacker modified the build automation tool to include backdoor code in all compiled applications.
Malicious code extracted the memory map from /proc/7890/maps to calculate base addresses of loaded modules.
LunarWeb can identify shared resources in compromised environments.
During C0027, Scattered Spider leveraged compromised credentials from victim users to authenticate to Azure tenants.
Uroburos has the ability to move data between its kernel and user mode components, generally using named pipes.
HOPLIGHT has utilized Zlib compression to obfuscate the communications payload.
The threat actor examined HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Nls\Language registry values to identify the system's location.
By querying cdn-lookup.com, the adversary obtained information about the victim's CDN configuration.
The attacker used AWS CLI to upload sensitive files directly to an S3 bucket under their control.
Saint Bot has relied on users to click on a malicious link delivered via a spearphishing.
The script used 'shred -n 3 -z -u' to securely overwrite and delete sensitive configuration files.
During Frankenstein, the threat actors established persistence through a scheduled task using the command: `/Create /F /SC DAILY /ST 09:00 /TN WinUpdate /TR`, named "WinUpdate"
Malicious PowerShell commands modified HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\EventLog registry keys to disable Windows event logging.
The malware manipulated Windows Security interface elements to conceal real-time protection status changes.
The adversary altered MX records in the DNS configuration to intercept and capture corporate email communications.

Kazuar is obfuscated using the open source ConfuserEx protector. Kazuar also obfuscates the name of created files/folders/mutexes and encrypts debug messages written to log files using the Rijndael cipher.
The attacker deleted HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\Default to remove evidence of RDP connections.
MuddyWater has exploited the Office vulnerability CVE-2017-0199 for execution.
The threat actor performed an nmap network sweep to identify potential entry points and service vulnerabilities.
NinjaCopy enabled direct sector reading to access password files without file system monitoring.
POWERTON can install a Registry Run key for persistence.
Attackers modified URI endpoints by appending random query parameters to disguise C2 traffic patterns.
The malicious program called malloc in the target process to allocate memory for the injected payload.
The threat actor abused PowerShell via Azure Automation to run 'Invoke-Expression' on remote systems.
CrackMapExec can brute force credential authentication by using a supplied list of usernames and a single password.
Saint Bot has injected its DLL component into `EhStorAurhn.exe`.
The malware executed a malicious executable that continuously spawned processes to exhaust system resources and maintain denial of service.
Malicious firmware was injected into a wireless router during the supply chain to create persistent access.
The malware invoked DCShadow.exe to create a backdoor user account in Active Directory.
Sardonic PowerShell scripts can be encrypted with RC4 and compressed using Gzip.
The Bazar loader is used to download and execute the Bazar backdoor.
Malware executed 'net user support P@ssw0rd! /add' to establish a hidden administrative account on the compromised system.
Nmap was employed to scan the client's network and gather information about active hosts.

Sandworm Team has researched software code to enable supply-chain operations, most notably for the 2017 NotPetya attack. Sandworm Team also collected a list of computers using specific software as part of its targeting efforts.
ArcaneDoor included multiple instances of file deletion or removal during execution and other adversary actions.
HAFNIUM has collected e-mail addresses for users they intended to target.
Attackers collected financial documents and stored them on a pastebin-like service.
The threat actor tampered with the software compilation pipeline to inject malicious code into the final distributed application.
APT41 cloned victim user Git repositories during intrusions.
The malware programmatically accessed the Keychain at ~/Library/Keychains/ to extract sensitive authentication data.

ThiefQuest invokes time call to check the system's time, executes a sleep command, invokes a second time call, and then compares the time difference between the two time calls and the amount of time the system slept to identify the sandbox.

The malware tampered with HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Microsoft\Cryptography\OID\EncodingType 0\CryptSIPDllGetSignedDataMsg\{C689AAB8-8E78-11D0-8C47-00C04FC295EE} to circumvent digital signature validation.
Malicious code was used to collect sensitive files from an Amazon S3 bucket containing customer data.
The malware injected its command and control module as malicious.dll into explorer.exe to blend with normal traffic.
The attacker exploited delegated administrator permissions in the third-party service provider's account to gain administrative access to the target tenant.
A systemd service file was written to disk to establish persistence and run payloads on boot.
Moonstone Sleet has created social media accounts to interact with victims.
During the 2015 Ukraine Electric Power Attack, Sandworm Team installed a modified Dropbear SSH client as the backdoor to target systems.
Troll Stealer compresses stolen data prior to exfiltration.
FIN13 has created hidden files and folders within a compromised Linux system `/tmp` directory. FIN13 also has used `attrib.exe` to hide gathered local host information.
The malware executed PowerShell with encoded commands to maintain persistence on the compromised system.
The backdoor established persistence by writing a timer unit 'security-scan.timer' that triggered the payload daily.
Attor's dispatcher can inject itself into running processes to gain higher privileges and to evade detection.
The adversary wrote a malicious version of 'crypt32.dll' into a directory with higher search precedence.
The ransomware employed 'tree E:\ /f' to enumerate all files and directories on the connected USB storage.
During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29, used `AUDITPOL` to prevent the collection of audit logs.
The adversary queried certificate authority records to map the victim's digital infrastructure.
Calisto collects Keychain storage data and copies those passwords/tokens to a file.
The malware signed up for a Tutanota email service using randomly generated credentials for command and control.
Compromised credentials were used to query the GetCredentialType API and validate email addresses for subsequent attacks.
NightClub can use `GetForegroundWindow` to enumerate the active window.
Stuxnet used xp_cmdshell to store and execute SQL code.
Mustang Panda has used RAR to create password-protected archives of collected documents prior to exfiltration.
Using pth.exe with stolen credentials, the adversary authenticated to a remote file server.
The threat actor executed 'malware.exe' to create a resilient backdoor connection.
LockBit 3.0 will not affect machines with language settings matching a defined exlusion list of mainly Eastern European languages.
The threat actor modified StartupParameters.plist to include their backdoor in the boot sequence.
The attacker executed malicious code via 'rundll32.exe dfshim.dll,ShOpenVerbApplication' to bypass application whitelisting.
A Lazarus Group malware sample encodes data with base64.
BITTER has exploited CVE-2021-1732 for privilege escalation.
The adversary modified /var/spool/cron/crontabs/root to execute a keylogger every 5 minutes and capture sensitive user input.
Kwampirs collects a list of domain groups with the command net localgroup /domain.
A backdoor was implanted by modifying the Windows SSH client binary.
The trojan modified malicious.exe by adding junk code and renaming functions to obscure its true purpose.
HEXANE has run `whoami` on compromised machines to identify the current user.
A PowerShell script invoked GetLocaleInfoW to determine the victim's regional settings and language.
The malware added a new SAML token signing certificate to the Active Directory Federation Services trust policy.
Malicious code was injected into a popular open-source library dependency used by thousands of applications.
The adversary leveraged the Azure Serial Console to troubleshoot and manipulate a virtual machine's operating system directly.
The backdoor downloaded a remote code execution exploit from http://drop-server.org/rce_tool.zip for initial access.
The backdoor utilized raw sockets to send command data directly through the network stack.
The threat actor dropped a trojanized .dylib file that mimicked a legitimate system library name to achieve persistence.

Duqu creates a new service that loads a malicious driver when the system starts. When Duqu is active, the operating system believes that the driver is legitimate, as it has been signed with a valid private key.
CHOPSTICK may modify Registry keys to store RC4 encrypted configuration information.
The adversary utilized WinRM to remotely query system information from the targeted Windows machine.
The infection routine located Google Chrome browser folders to collect browsing history and cookies.
A malicious Excel document showed a 'Security Warning - Enable Content' prompt that ran PowerShell commands when enabled.
Attackers leveraged libzip to compress multiple stolen document files into a password-protected archive.
The threat actor wrote a malicious DLL into the memory of the legitimate notepad.exe process.
njRAT can search a list of running processes for Tr.exe.
Gazer can establish persistence by creating a .lnk file in the Start menu or by modifying existing .lnk files to execute the malware through cmd.exe.
The threat actor ran 'cmd.exe /c dir \\192.168.1.100\c$\users\*.doc' to search for Word documents in user directories.
Revenge RAT uses the Forfiles utility to execute commands on the system.
RTM has relied on users opening malicious email attachments, decompressing the attached archive, and double-clicking the executable within.
The malicious script set COR_PROFILER_PATH to point to a compromised DLL for persistence in .NET environments.
BRONZE BUTLER has used net time to check the local time on a target system.
Versa Director Zero Day Exploitation intercepted and harvested credentials from user logins to compromised devices.
The malware scanned /proc/ directory to identify running processes for potential injection targets.
The threat actor used specialized tools to modify inventory management software and falsify stock levels.
Using 'net group /domain', the adversary listed all available domain groups.
The malware dropped a credential-harvesting DLL into the Windows temp folder.
CURIUM has created dedicated email accounts for use with tools such as IMAPLoader.
ngrok has been used by threat actors to configure servers for data exfiltration.
Raspberry Robin uses newly-registered domains containing only a few characters for command and controll purposes, such as "v0.cx".
Storm-1811 has used `cacls.exe` via batch script to modify file and directory permissions in victim environments.
Malware wrote a custom Outlook form to the user's Forms Library for execution upon Outlook startup.
The attacker modified the home page to show a ransom note demanding payment.
APT18 obfuscates strings in the payload.
Doki’s container was configured to bind the host root directory.
The trojan connected to an open WiFi access point to upload stolen financial information.
Fox Kitten has accessed victim security and IT environments and Microsoft Teams to mine valuable information.
The reconnaissance tool systematically queried the Autodiscover service to collect valid email addresses from the target domain.
Caterpillar WebShell has a module to use a rootkit on a system.
HermeticWiper can compress 32-bit and 64-bit driver files with the Lempel-Ziv algorithm.
The malware employed a DGA algorithm to create new domains for fallback communication channels.
Malware employed ImpersonateLoggedOnUser to assume the privileges of an active user session.
The backdoor injected malicious.dll into svchost.exe to elevate privileges and evade detection.
CMSTP.exe was exploited to run a malicious payload embedded in a specially crafted connection profile.
Malware was stored on IPFS using a content-addressed hash to ensure persistence and availability.
Sensitive documents were collected and sent via unencrypted HTTP requests to a remote server.
MoustachedBouncer has used JavaScript to deliver malware hosted on HTML pages.
The malicious code called CreateProcess with special flags to create a child process undetected by security tools.
The threat actor ran a PowerShell script that called Get-GlobalAddressList to harvest employee email addresses from Exchange.
Sidewinder has named malicious files rekeywiz.exe to match the name of a legitimate Windows executable.
The attacker used the PutBucketLifecycle API to configure immediate deletion of all objects in the S3 bucket.
Leafminer used Microsoft’s Sysinternals tools to gather detailed information about remote systems.
A high-volume UDP flood was sent to the gaming server on port 27015, causing severe lag.
The adversary replaced system DLLs with malicious.dll to hijack LSASS driver loading mechanisms.
PoshC2 contains an implementation of Mimikatz to gather credentials from memory.
MCMD can use scheduled tasks for persistence.
Adversaries exploited the user registration feature by automating thousands of sign-up attempts that overwhelmed the database.
The threat actor altered Autorun.inf to include commands that would run malware.exe upon insertion.
A Threat Group-3390 tool can use WMI to execute a binary.
Storm-1811 has locally staged captured credentials for subsequent manual exfiltration.
Storm-1811 has created Microsoft Teams accounts that spoof IT support and helpdesk members for use in application and voice phishing.
Remexi utilizes scheduled tasks as a persistence mechanism.
The attacker collected system information via unencrypted HTTP POST requests.
The intrusion tool executed pwdumpx.exe to obtain password hashes for offline cracking.
Snip3 has the ability to detect Windows Sandbox, VMWare, or VirtualBox by querying `Win32_ComputerSystem` to extract the `Manufacturer` string.
The malware executed VBA p-code stored in the document's metadata to bypass macro security warnings.
APT38 has used cron to create pre-scheduled and periodic background jobs on a Linux system.
The malicious payload malicious.dll was dropped into the user's AppData folder for proxy execution.
The threat actor modified file permissions using icacls to allow unauthorized users to execute privileged scripts.
Dridex can use multiple layers of proxy servers to hide terminal nodes in its infrastructure.
The adversary used 'history -c' to wipe command history after executing privilege escalation commands.
Cobalt Strike can use Window admin shares (C$ and ADMIN$) for lateral movement.
The malware employed icacls to remove inherited permissions from a critical system file.
Ransomware leveraged weak default credentials on the Administrator account to deploy its payload.
AppleJeus has used shell scripts to execute commands after installation and set persistence mechanisms.
Earth Lusca has registered domains, intended to look like legitimate target domains, that have been used in watering hole attacks.
Malware exploited firewall NAT settings to redirect internal traffic to external C2 servers.
QakBot can use obfuscated and encoded scripts.
Mimikatz.exe was deployed to collect Kerberos tickets from the compromised system.
Using the TxF API, the attacker modified the target process's memory to evade detection mechanisms.
dsquery can be used to gather information on domain trusts with dsquery * -filter "(objectClass=trustedDomain)" -attr *.
The malware modified the network device's system image to downgrade SSH encryption from AES-256 to DES.
Kevin can hide the current window from the targeted user via the `ShowWindow` API function.
KOCTOPUS will perform UAC bypass either through fodhelper.exe or eventvwr.exe.
Using Mimikatz, the attacker dumped LSASS memory to obtain plaintext passwords from active sessions.
Meteor can fill a victim's files and directories with zero-bytes in replacement of real content before deleting them.
The ransomware encrypted all communication channels with its C2 server using custom cryptographic protocols.
A compromised VIB payload was written to the hypervisor to maintain command and control connectivity.
Dok adds admin ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL to the /etc/sudoers file.
The attacker modified the ADFS relying party trust to redirect authentication to a compromised server.
Lotus Blossom has installed tools such as Sagerunex by writing them to the Windows registry.
Command and control communications were hidden within SSH sessions to corporate jump servers, evading network monitoring.
The backdoor stored its configuration data in reserved disk space that standard file system utilities cannot access.
The attacker used pwdumpx.exe to extract password hashes from the local SAM database.
BabyShark has the ability to decode downloaded files prior to execution.
The malware stored encrypted data chunks at https://c2.com/chunk after splitting the exfiltrated information.
The attacker wrote malicious.cs to disk as part of the payload deployment.
The attacker dropped 'crypted_payload.dat' with encrypted shellcode to evade antivirus scanning.
The threat actor altered the Windows Security Center interface to hide actual security service failures.
ECCENTRICBANDWAGON can use cmd to execute commands on a victim’s machine.
CharmPower can use `wmic` to gather information from a system.
INC Ransomware has the ability to change the background wallpaper image to display the ransom note.
Explosive has encrypted communications with the RC4 method.
IcedID has the ability to identify Workgroup membership.
The attacker searched YouTube for corporate training videos that might reveal internal processes.
Chimera has used WMIC to execute remote commands.
Lumma Stealer has gained initial execution through victims opening malicious executable files embedded in zip archives, and MSI files within RAR files.
The infection employed vssadmin resize shadow to manipulate shadow storage before data theft.
Attackers hid command and control instructions inside a PNG image using steganography techniques.
Helminth establishes persistence by creating a shortcut.
FIN7 used JavaScript scripts to help perform tasks on the victim's machine.
BADHATCH has the ability to execute a malicious DLL by injecting into `explorer.exe` on a compromised machine.
Ramsay has masqueraded as a JPG image file.
The spyware leveraged the built-in microphone to record sensitive conversations in the office environment.
C2 messages were gzipped by the threat actor to bypass content inspection systems.
GreyEnergy has a module to inject a PE binary into a remote process.
Squirrelwaffle has been executed using `rundll32.exe`.
The script performed a recursive directory search with 'dir /s *.xml' to find GPP files with embedded credentials.
The attacker removed linker metadata from the compiled binary to obscure function names.
Bazar can use Winlogon Helper DLL to establish persistence.
LookBack has used VBA macros in Microsoft Word attachments to drop additional files to the host.
Star Blizzard has bypassed multi-factor authentication on victim email accounts by using session cookies stolen using EvilGinx.
FlawedAmmyy can collect clipboard data.
VersaMem was installed through exploitation of CVE-2024-39717 in Versa Director servers.
TrickBot can implant malicious code into a compromised device's firmware.
An attacker modified HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Lsa\\Authentication Packages to load a malicious DLL.
The threat actor edited the AppCertDLLs registry value to load a compromised DLL from a user-writeable location.
Attackers abused Visual Studio Code's Live Share extension to gain unauthorized remote access to developer workstations.
Sandworm Team has scanned network infrastructure for vulnerabilities as part of its operational planning.
Regin appears to have functionality to modify remote Registry information.
WindTail has the ability to use the macOS built-in zip utility to archive files.
LockBit 3.0 can attempt a CMSTP UAC bypass if it does not have administrative privileges.
The malware created a CronJob to periodically execute a container that harvests credentials from other pods.
Brute Ratel C4 can use LDAP queries and `nltest /domain_trusts` for domain trust discovery.
The malware executed at.exe to run a data exfiltration tool at midnight.
OSInfo queries the registry to look for information about Terminal Services.
Malicious scripts were used to query Smart Install clients for network topology information.
The malware tampered with the hosts file to intercept and drop packets destined for multi-factor authentication servers.
During C0017, APT41 established persistence by loading malicious libraries via modifications to the Import Address Table (IAT) within legitimate Microsoft binaries.
The threat actor modified the cryptographic driver to inject a backdoor into encrypted channels.
Small Sieve can obtain the id of a logged in user.

Pikabot further decrypts information embedded via steganography using AES-CBC with the same 32 bit key as initial XOR operations combined with the first 16 bytes of the encrypted data as an initialization vector. Other Pikabot variants include encrypted, chunked sections of the stage 2 payload in the initial loader .text section before decrypting and assembling these during execution.
The ransomware stored its encryption keys in binary format within custom Windows Registry values to avoid detection.
Malware exploited CreateProcessWithTokenW to create a privileged process using a duplicated token from a system service.
The adversary abused network CLI with 'no cdp run' to disable Cisco Discovery Protocol and avoid detection.
The attacker wrote a PowerShell script that adds random padding to network traffic to avoid signature-based detection.
GLOOXMAIL communicates to servers operated by Google using the Jabber/XMPP protocol.
The attacker renamed 'regedit.exe' to 'gpedit.exe' using RTLO character to disguise the system tool.
PLATINUM has attempted to get users to open malicious files by sending spearphishing emails with attachments to victims.
The payload was compiled with modified settings to remove debugging information.
Password stealer and NTLM stealer modules in CozyCar harvest stored credentials from the victim, including credentials used as part of Windows NTLM user authentication.
Stuxnet uses an RPC server that contains a file dropping routine and support for payload version updates for P2P communications within a victim network.
The threat actor used 'net user support $ecurePass123 /add' to create a secondary credentialed access point for future operations.
Mongall can use Base64 to encode information sent to its C2.
Rogue software was copied to a team collaboration folder disguised as a legitimate document.
Mimikatz.exe was executed to dump NTLM hashes from the local security authority.
Hancitor has used verclsid.exe to download and execute a malicious script.
The attacker used modified Base64 encoding with swapped characters to obfuscate command and control traffic.
XLoader can create scheduled tasks for persistence.
Inception has used VBScript to execute malicious commands and payloads.
CreepyDrive can use OneDrive for C2.
The threat actor edited rc.common to launch a cryptocurrency miner during system startup.

Line Runner intercepts HTTP requests to the victim Cisco ASA, looking for a request with a 32-character, victim dependent parameter. If that parameter matches a value in the malware, a contained payload is then written to a Lua script and executed.
Malware established persistence by using reg add to overwrite HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID\{ABCDEF01-2345-6789-ABCD-EF0123456789}\InprocServer32 with a path to its payload.
The threat actor executed a malicious extension installer to establish persistent control.
Malicious actors modified the firewall ruleset to allow unauthorized traffic between segmented networks.
During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group lured users into executing a malicious link to disclose private account information or provide initial access.
A malicious DLL file named 'helper.dll' was written to the company's shared storage location.
A malicious script was created to automatically provision cloud instances in different regions to avoid detection.
Cadelspy has the ability to record audio from the compromised host.
The adversary placed a concealed recording device in the executive's office to collect voice data during strategic meetings.
The adversary ran 'icacls C:\ProgramData\backdoor.exe /inheritance:e' to remove inherited permissions and maintain exclusive access.
Gamaredon Group has compiled the source code for a downloader directly on the infected system using the built-in Microsoft.CSharp.CSharpCodeProvider class.
The attacker used a compromised OAuth application to maintain persistent access to the victim's social media accounts.
XCSSET will compress entire ~/Desktop folders excluding all .git folders, but only if the total data size is under 200MB.
Goopy has the ability to exfiltrate documents from infected systems.
DarkTortilla can implement the `kernel32.dll` Sleep function to delay execution for up to 300 seconds before implementing persistence or processing an addon package.
Fox Kitten has used WizTree to obtain network files and directory listings.

BOLDMOVE contains a watchdog-like feature that monitors a particular file for modification. If modification is detected, the legitimate file is backed up and replaced with a trojanized file to allow for persistence through likely system upgrades.
The attacker leveraged MailSniper to search for emails containing financial transaction details.
Saint Bear has leveraged vulnerabilities in client applications such as CVE-2017-11882 in Microsoft Office to enable code execution in victim environments.
FIN7 spear phishing campaigns have included malicious Word documents with DDE execution.
APT38 has used a custom MBR wiper named BOOTWRECK, which will initiate a system reboot after wiping the victim's MBR.
Metamorfo has downloaded a zip file for execution on the system.
The intrusion involved installing TinyPilot alongside legitimate remote access tools for redundancy.
During APT28 Nearest Neighbor Campaign, APT28 accessed volume shadow copies through executing vssadmin in order to dump the NTDS.dit file.
RedCurl has downloaded 7-Zip to decompress password protected archives.
Gelsemium can bypass UAC to elevate process privileges on a compromised host.
PowerShower has the ability to save and execute VBScript.
Attackers exploited the ADMIN$ share to deploy a disk-wiping utility across the network.
esentutl can be used to read and write alternate data streams.
The attacker abused svchost.exe to run a malicious DLL that created backdoor access.
FatDuke can identify the MAC address on the target computer.
ABK can extract a malicious Portable Executable (PE) from a photo.
The malware exploited libpcap libraries to capture network packets and identify unused ports for covert communication.
Some Orz versions have an embedded DLL known as MockDll that uses Process Hollowing and regsvr32 to execute another payload.
The adversary altered the cloud firewall configuration to bypass network segmentation controls.
By compromising an Office 365 partner account, the threat actor was able to escalate privileges across multiple customer environments.
APT32 modified Windows Services to ensure PowerShell scripts were loaded on the system. APT32 also creates a Windows service to establish persistence.
MgBot includes modules for collecting files from local systems based on a given set of properties and filenames.
A script ran 'cacls C:\config.ini /E /P Everyone:R' to modify the ACL and grant read permissions to everyone.
OilRig has exfiltrated data via Microsoft Exchange and over FTP separately from its primary C2 channel over DNS.
S-Type may create a temporary user on the system named `Lost_{Unique Identifier}` with the password `pond~!@6”{Unique Identifier}`.
DirBuster was used with extension filtering to specifically find PHP files and scripts.
The persistence mechanism involved creating a new plist file in the user's LaunchAgents directory to trigger execution upon login.
Sibot has been executed via MSHTA application.
JSS Loader can download and execute JavaScript files.
Carbanak actors used legitimate credentials of banking employees to perform operations that sent them millions of dollars.
Spica can use JSON over WebSockets for C2 communications.
The malware performed a DNS query to resolve the domain name of the target organization.
Egregor contains functionality to query the local/system time.
Pysa has used Python scripts to deploy ransomware.
During Operation MidnightEclipse, threat actors used SMB to pivot internally in victim networks.
Using icacls, the threat actor removed all access restrictions from the user's home folder.

During AppleJeus's installation process, it uses `postinstall` scripts to extract a hidden plist from the application's `/Resources` folder and execute the `plist` file as a Launch Daemon with elevated permissions.
Leviathan has sent spearphishing emails with links, often using a fraudulent lookalike domain and stolen branding.
The attacker abused VMware Workstation's snapshot feature to create hidden virtual machine states containing persistence mechanisms.
Malicious browser extensions hook password manager APIs to exfiltrate stored credentials silently.
The attacker used iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator to create temporary credentials for accessing protected cloud resources.
Machete’s collected files are exfiltrated automatically to remote servers.
A compromised application employed CreateProcessWithTokenW to impersonate a high-privilege user and execute malicious code.
Malicious actors modified the firewall rules to allow unauthorized traffic between segmented networks.
APT39 has used Ncrack to reveal credentials.
The malicious script queried the domain controller to identify accounts with weak password policies.
The ransomware removed its dropper component after installing the main payload.
BLINDINGCAN has been delivered by phishing emails containing malicious Microsoft Office documents.
Attackers used bind mounts to overlay a malicious library directory over a legitimate one, hiding their payloads.
Mimikatz’s LSADUMP::DCSync and KERBEROS::PTT modules implement the three steps required to extract the krbtgt account hash and create/use Kerberos tickets.
The threat actor leveraged Windows Admin Shares to overwrite system binaries with corrupt data.
GrimAgent can use an AES key to encrypt C2 communications.
The attacker cleared the System event log using wevtutil to hide unauthorized access attempts.
The malicious script transmitted encoded commands through HTTP Referer headers during beaconing.
The threat actor developed 'fuzzer_pro.exe' to identify buffer overflow vulnerabilities in target software.
Malicious code exploited PowerShell.exe by using legacy version to disable security controls.
Malware was disguised by writing malicious PHP code within a file named 'login.php' to blend with legitimate web application files.
The attacker sent phishing emails disguised as cloud service notifications to harvest employee credentials.
Malware injected a malicious DLL into explorer.exe using mavinject.exe to maintain persistence.
Crimson has the ability to discover pluggable/removable drives to extract files from.
